topic: World History Topic: Causes & Effects of 20th-Century Wars
subject: history

items.correct: A combination of moral campaigning, slave resistance and rebellions (especially Haiti and Jamaica), and the shifting economic interest of industrial capitalism.
items.misconception: Wilberforce single-handedly persuaded Parliament to end slavery out of pure morality.
items.random_wrong: Slavery was abolished because cotton was no longer profitable.
items.why: Students inherit the heroic Wilberforce narrative. Recent scholarship emphasises slave revolts (Haiti, Demerara, Jamaica) and economic shifts — abolition was not a gift from above.

items.q: What was the theory of the four humours?
items.correct: Disease was caused by imbalance of blood, phlegm, yellow bile and black bile; treatment aimed to rebalance them via bloodletting, purging or diet. It dominated for nearly 2,000 years.
items.misconception: Medieval doctors knew about germs but used humours because they had no medicine.
items.random_wrong: The four humours were invented by Pasteur to explain bacteria.
items.why: Humoral theory was a coherent system from Hippocrates and Galen, not ignorance. Students retroject germ theory backwards, missing why educated people believed it for so long.

items.q: Why were railways transformative for Victorian Britain?
items.correct: They cut transport costs and times, integrated national markets, enabled commuting and mass leisure travel, and standardised time zones — reshaping economy and society after 1830.
items.misconception: Railways were transformative mainly because they were faster than horses.
items.random_wrong: Railways were transformative because they were the first form of public transport.
items.why: Speed mattered but the deeper change was economic integration: prices converged, goods moved, towns grew around lines. Students focus on novelty rather than systemic impact.

items.q: Which 1938 conference ceded the Sudetenland to Germany?
items.why: It's Munich. “Locarno” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who signed the 1939 non-aggression pact for the USSR?
items.why: Correct answer: Molotov. “Litvinov” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what month and year did Hitler remilitarise the Rhineland?
items.why: The real figure is 1936 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
